This won't be an issue. The idea is that I export (in a pythonic/include/.../...hpp) the ``to_python()`` function and it gets specialized in the relevant header. If you want to call a specific ``to_python`` specialization, just call the good one and it will be automagically dispatched thanks to the template specialization mechanism. The only problem that could appear would be template use before specialization. We just need to respect the correct include order to by pass this.

The benfit ? cleaner include files as only the interface is exposed there. For instance sometimes the ``namespace impl`` stuff are also exported, which should be avoided (that's how I understand the ``impl`` namespace.)

The method was written in such a way that if user calls `to_channels_first()` two or more times accidently on the same data tensor, then the method should return the ouput in always `channels_first` format. If I remove that check, then there is a channel conversion mismatch if user calls `to_channels_first()` multiple times.

```python
For instance:

Before check:
x_input = A Numpy tensor of shape (10, 3, 32, 32)
x_input = to_channels_first(x_input) -> output shape (10, 32, 3, 32)

After check:
x_input = A Numpy tensor of shape (10, 3, 32, 32)
x_input = to_channels_first(x_input) -> output shape (10, 3, 32, 32)
```

So, by keeping that shape check, we won't face such issues. However, as u mentioned, it will fail when image dimension is `(2, 2)` or `(1, 1)` or values smaller than channels in general. This would be possible if the data is synthetic and also, there is no other way to check based on tensors dimensions and impossible to cover all the edge cases. So, I have removed the check and make it more generic and assume user aware of it's data dimensions.

> > It's obvious that a new version failing to parse a format that was parsed before is a breaking change.

> I think that depends on whether the timestamp being parsed is actually ISO 8601 conforming.

I actually specifically think that it *doesn't* depend on this; if somebody's using the library to parse non-conforming datetimes, then the library ceasing to parse them is a breaking change even if it's also a bugfix. I figure there's a reason that https://semver.org/ specifically uses the phrase "backwards compatible bug fix" (rather than just "bug fix") in several places, and that's that not all bug fixes are backwards compatible.

But it's all fairly open to interpretation!

They're not that different, just a `[]` added to every call. `owners=[]` is an anti-pattern because when owners goes to the default, it's always the same list. So if you append to the list after accepting a default argument, the default is no longer empty. So it's a choice between doing `owners=None` and starting the function with

```
if owners is None:
    owners = []
```

I think that changes to domain parsing can be reverted and logic similar to below can be used in `webroot.py` to achieve the same effect. There are a couple ways to do this. Basically, you need to keep track of the previous `webroot-path` flag. Then when you encounter a new `webroot-path` flag, you know you will use that webroot for all new domains found in `config.domains`. The very last `webroot-path` flag will require special treatment and will have to be applied outside of argument parsing, but I believe this can be done nicely using a function to avoid duplicated code.

> I mainly wanted to avoid having complex data structures (e.g. arrays of objects) as props because they're harder to validate and document.
That makes sense, it just seems counter intuitive to the idea behind a child component. We have to pass in the same props using the same grandchildren over and over, which makes the template feel more verbose than it needs to be.

 > Would they actually reduce code repetition?
Yes, because you'd only specify the granchild component once for every item in the array. However, you'd have to pass in the array via a prop and the grandchild component via a slot, making it kind of complex.

> I'm admittedly not the sharpest tool in the shed
Nah. It took me a lot of experimenting and rereading to understand how they worked. Christian had some trouble with it too. Maybe this is reason enough to forego the approach.

Basically, adding `scope` to a slot while it's being used in a parent passes up the scope of the child to the parent (only within the slot) which might be anti-pattern.

Correct me if I am wrong - but this is going to load **everything** that has subclassed FrontEndAssetHook, right? So, there is no possibility for differentiation here, in terms of telling different assets to be loaded on different templates.

The original intent of the `{% base_frontend_sync %}` and the `{% base_frontend_async %}` tags was not this, but rather to expose hooks that could be used to place frontend assets specifically in the `base.html`.

For other templates (such as `learn.html`) other hooks would have to be defined in the relevant app, that would allow frontend assets to be loaded only in `learn.html`.
